title: Trusted proxy headers (X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Proto)
description:
1. When cooked runs behind nginx/caddy, all request logs show the proxy IP (127.0.0.1) instead of the real client IP
2. Add a --trusted-proxies flag that accepts CIDRs or IPs (e.g. 127.0.0.1, 10.0.0.0/8)
3. When set, cooked reads X-Forwarded-For to extract the real client IP for logging
4. Also read X-Forwarded-Proto to detect https when behind a TLS-terminating proxy, which affects --base-url auto-detection
5. Without this, operators cannot correlate cooked logs with client requests
close_reason: Added --trusted-proxies flag with X-Forwarded-For client IP extraction

title: Git-aware mode
notes: Won't fix — cooked is URL-agnostic by design. It fetches whatever URL you give it without knowing about git forges. Coupling to cgit/gitea/forgejo URL patterns adds complexity for unclear value. Users already construct the correct raw-file URL.

title: Fix TestSetup_JSONOutput coverage theater — test Setup() not stdlib
description: logging_test.go:12-37 never calls Setup(), tests slog.NewJSONHandler directly. If Setup() had a bug (wrong level, wrong output), this test wouldn't catch it. Fix: test Setup() directly by verifying it sets slog.Default() and produces JSON.

title: Add reStructuredText (.rst) rendering
notes:
Won't fix — no pure-Go RST parser exists.

All current renderers (goldmark, libasciidoc, go-org, chroma) are pure Go, keeping the binary self-contained. Adding RST would require shelling out to Python/docutils or Pandoc, breaking the core 'fully self-contained binary' guarantee.

The only Go RST library (gorst by nicholasgasior) is abandoned and unusable — no directives, no cross-references. RST adoption is declining; .md and .adoc cover the same audience.

When cooked is on a different origin than the upstream (e.g., 127.0.0.1:8080 vs intranet host), the browser blocks it due to CORS. Fix: proxy raw content through cooked via a server-side raw endpoint instead of fetching directly from the browser.

title: Migrate from ARC Kubernetes runners to GitHub-hosted runners
description:
Move the release pipeline from self-hosted ARC Kubernetes runners to GitHub-hosted runners. This eliminates all ARC-specific workarounds (kaniko, container: requirements, app tokens for PR creation) and simplifies the workflow significantly.

## Why

GitHub-hosted runners are free for public repos (unlimited minutes, 4 vCPU/16GB RAM). ARC Kubernetes runners have persistent issues in 2026: no Docker daemon, container init failures, jobs stuck in Queued state, pod placement bugs. The operational complexity is not justified for an open-source project.

## What Changes

### release.yml simplification:
- Remove container: from every job (GitHub-hosted runners run on full VMs)
- Replace kaniko with standard docker/build-push-action (Docker daemon available)
- Remove actions/create-github-app-token (GITHUB_TOKEN can create PRs on hosted runners, unless org blocks it)
- Replace arc-runner-set with ubuntu-latest (or ubuntu-24.04)
- Remove GOFLAGS: -buildvcs=false (not needed outside containers)

### Docker job rewrite:
- Use docker/setup-buildx-action + docker/build-push-action
- Multi-arch support via QEMU (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64)
- Standard GHCR login via docker/login-action
- No more kaniko auth workarounds, git:// context URLs, or debug images

### Build matrix stays the same:
- linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64
- darwin/amd64 has ~1-2 years of relevance (macOS 27 drops Intel fall 2026)
- No 32-bit ARM (Pi ecosystem moved to 64-bit, ARMv6/7 EOL 2026)
- No older macOS concern (Go 1.25+ minimum is macOS 12 Monterey)

## Research Summary (2026-02-07)

### GitHub-hosted runners:
- 39% price cut January 2026
- Free unlimited minutes for public repos on standard runners
- Specs: 4 vCPU, 16GB RAM (up to 96 vCPU larger runners available)
- Docker daemon available out of the box
- macOS runners also free for public repos if needed later

### ARC still problematic:
- Jobs stuck in Queued state after hours
- Pod placement issues (runner/worker on different nodes)
- K8s 1.33 user namespaces help but fundamental friction remains
- kaniko was archived June 2025 (community forks continue, v1.26.4 Jan 2026)
- BuildKit on K8s still needs hostUsers:false + privileged:true (infra-level config)

### macOS binaries:
- Cross-compilation from Linux works fine for pure Go (no CGO)
- Unsigned binaries get Gatekeeper warnings (developers know xattr workaround)
- Proper signing requires macOS runner + Apple Developer Account ($99/yr) — overkill for CLI
- darwin/amd64 still needed (Intel Macs exist) but macOS 27 (fall 2026) drops Intel

### ARM/Pi:
- Skip 32-bit ARM (GOARM=6/7) — ecosystem moved to 64-bit
- linux/arm64 covers Pi 4/5 and all ARM servers
- Major projects dropped armhf support in 2025-2026

## References
- See .local/RELEASE_LEARNINGS.md for ARC/kaniko lessons learned
- CERN rootless builds blog: kubernetes.web.cern.ch/blog/2025/06/19/rootless-container-builds-on-kubernetes/
- GitHub pricing: github.blog/changelog/2026-01-01-reduced-pricing-for-github-hosted-runners-usage/
- ARC issues: github.com/actions/actions-runner-controller/issues/2805
close_reason: All items complete: ARC→GitHub-hosted migration (c896b69), GITHUB_TOKEN auth (aaf6d85), multi-arch Docker amd64+arm64 (8280fcf)

title: Directory listing support
notes:
Won't fix — scope creep for a rendering proxy.

cooked renders single files. Directory listing would turn it into a repository browser (detect HTML directory pages, rewrite links, handle subdirectories, breadcrumbs). The user already knows the upstream URL — cgit/gitea directory listings are navigable as-is. The value (click a .md and see it rendered) doesn't justify the complexity.

Current behavior: URLs without a recognized file extension return 415 'unsupported file type'. This is correct — cooked is a file renderer, not a directory browser.

title: Proxy images and assets through /_cooked/raw/
description:
1. Non-renderable files (images, fonts, etc.) in rendered markdown are rewritten to point directly at the upstream origin
2. When cooked runs on a different origin (cooked.example.com) than the upstream (cgit.internal), browsers block these cross-origin requests due to CORS / same-origin restrictions
3. The /_cooked/raw/ endpoint already exists and can proxy arbitrary upstream content without rendering
4. Fix: change internal/rewrite/rewrite.go to rewrite img src (and other non-renderable asset refs) through /_cooked/raw/ instead of pointing directly at upstream
5. This makes cooked a true rendering proxy where the browser never needs to reach the upstream directly
close_reason: Implemented: src attributes proxied through /_cooked/raw/, Content-Type passthrough, cache headers

title: Prometheus metrics endpoint
notes:
Deferred — useful but not needed yet.

cooked already emits structured JSON logs with all the data a /metrics endpoint would expose (upstream_ms, render_ms, cache status, content_type, bytes, status code). X-Cooked-* response headers also enable access-log-based dashboards.

If the air-gapped cluster runs Prometheus, this becomes a one-dependency feature (prometheus/client_golang) with real value: request counters, latency histograms, cache hit ratios. Reopen when Prometheus is confirmed in the deployment environment.

Metrics already available via logs: request count, cache hit/miss rate, upstream latency, render latency, error rate by status code, bytes served, content type distribution.

title: Run update-ca-certificates at container startup for runtime CA injection
description:
## Problem
cooked runs in air-gapped Kubernetes with trust-manager distributing custom CA certs as ConfigMaps. The current Dockerfile runs update-ca-certificates at build time only. Custom CAs mounted at runtime are never picked up because Go caches certs on first TLS call (sync.Once in crypto/x509).

## Root Cause
1. update-ca-certificates runs at image build time (Dockerfile line 29) — only system CAs baked in
2. At runtime, trust-manager mounts custom CA to /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/
3. Nobody runs update-ca-certificates again — Go reads the stale build-time bundle
4. Go caches certs on first TLS call (sync.Once) — no hot reload possible

## Key Constraints
- Cert files MUST have .crt extension (update-ca-certificates silently ignores .pem)
- /etc/ssl/certs/ must be writable (no readOnlyRootFilesystem without emptyDir)
- Volume mounts should use subPath to avoid wiping existing directory contents
- Go searches /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt first, stops at first file found

## Solution
Change entrypoint to a wrapper script that:
1. Runs update-ca-certificates (picks up certs mounted to /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/)
2. exec cooked

## Tasks
- [ ] Create entrypoint.sh wrapper script
- [ ] Update Dockerfile ENTRYPOINT to use entrypoint.sh
- [ ] Update README.md with runtime CA injection docs (Kubernetes volume mount pattern)

title: Decision: rejected feature ideas from user-perspective audit
description:
Features evaluated on 2026-04-03 by analyzing cooked from 10 user perspectives (platform eng, developer, SRE, security auditor, air-gapped admin, gitea integrator, manager, contributor, reverse proxy admin, monitoring builder). These were explicitly rejected with reasoning. Do not re-propose without new evidence.

1. Prometheus /metrics endpoint: overkill for a rendering proxy, structured JSON request logs with upstream_ms, render_ms, cache status are sufficient
2. Readiness probe /readyz with upstream check: if an upstream is offline, cooked should NOT fail health checks and get killed by k8s. /healthz (process alive) is the correct signal
3. CONTRIBUTING.md: solo project, CLAUDE.md covers architecture and conventions
4. Config validation mode (cooked --validate): just start the server and check logs, not worth a separate mode
5. Slow upstream warning log: already visible in upstream_ms field in every request log entry, adding a threshold-based warning is redundant

title: WI-15: HTTP handlers, routing, and middleware
notes: Plan: WI-15. Go 1.22+ ServeMux. Main handler pipeline. X-Cooked-* headers. Error pages.

title: Configurable X-Frame-Options for iframe embedding
description:
1. X-Frame-Options is hardcoded to DENY in internal/server/server.go
2. Gitea and cgit admins want to embed cooked output in iframes within their web UI (e.g. README tab renders via cooked instead of built-in renderer)
3. Add a flag like --frame-ancestors (using CSP frame-ancestors instead of X-Frame-Options which is deprecated)
4. Options: none (default, same as DENY), self (same-origin), or specific origins
5. Small change, unlocks a real integration pattern for self-hosted git forges

title: Allowlist CIDR ranges don't match hostnames that resolve to private IPs
description: When --allowed-upstreams includes a CIDR like 10.0.0.0/8, hostnames that resolve to IPs in that range are still blocked. The Allows() method only checks CIDRs against IP-literal hosts (net.ParseIP), never resolving hostnames via DNS. Fix: resolve hostname to IPs and check those against CIDR ranges.

title: F-09: Redact upstream URLs in headers and logs
description: Strip query/fragment/userinfo from X-Cooked-Upstream header. Add redactUpstream helper.
close_reason: Implemented and tested in security remediation session

close_reason: Wired logging.LogRequest into server loggingMiddleware, reads X-Cooked-* headers for upstream/cache/render_ms/upstream_ms/content_type fields. Replaced duplicate responseCapture with logging.ByteCountingWriter. Removed unused logging.Middleware.

title: Remove dead nil-check branch in allowlist fuzzer
description: allowlist_test.go:266-268 — when a == nil, calling a.Allows(host) always returns true (nil receiver), so the !a.Allows(host) condition is always false. This is dead code masquerading as a fuzz assertion.
